title: Gates as Tiny Decision Makers
concept: Physical switches and circuits are literal, physical logic gates — a closed switch means true, an open switch means false, and how the switches are wired together decides the outcome.

exercises.prompt: Recap: A truth table for a gate with 2 inputs always has exactly how many rows?
exercises.explain: Right! Now let's make gates physical — with actual wires and switches you could touch.

cards.title: The stairwell light problem
cards.body: Picture a stairwell light with one switch at the top of the stairs and one at the bottom. Either person should be able to turn the light on — nobody wants to walk down a dark staircase just because someone else flipped their switch off.

cards.title: Switches ARE logic gates
cards.body: A closed switch lets electricity — think of it as "true" — flow through; an open switch blocks it ("false"). Wiring several switches together in a circuit is a physical, hands-on way of building an actual logic gate.

cards.title: Switches are like valves in a pipe
cards.body: Wire two switches in a row (in series), and both must be closed for current to flow — that's an AND gate. Wire two switches side by side (in parallel), and either one closing lets current through — that's an OR gate. Water through pipes behaves exactly the same way.

cards.title: Solving the stairwell light
cards.body: Wire the two stairwell switches in parallel: flipping either one closes a path for the current. That's a real-world OR gate — "top switch OR bottom switch" turns the light on.

cards.title: A switch isn't a tiny computer
cards.body: It's tempting to think a single switch already "thinks." It doesn't — a switch just physically opens or closes a path. A computer is billions of these simple switches wired together, not one switch being clever.

cards.title: Series vs. parallel wiring
cards.body: How you wire switches decides which gate you get:

left.text:
Both switches must close
e.g. porch light needs power AND a night sensor

right.text:
Either switch can close
e.g. stairwell light, top OR bottom switch

exercises.prompt: Two switches are wired in parallel to control one lamp. Which gate does this circuit behave like?
exercises.explain: Correct! In parallel wiring, either switch closing lets current flow — exactly how an OR gate behaves.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Wiring two switches in series (one after another) means BOTH must be closed for current to flow — behaving like an AND gate.
exercises.explain: True! In series, current only has one path, and every switch on that path must be closed.

exercises.sentence: In a circuit, a closed switch represents ___, and an open switch represents false.
exercises.explain: A closed switch lets current flow, which we treat as "true" — the open switch blocks it, or "false".

exercises.prompt: Which real circuit setup is behaving like an AND gate?

options.text: A porch light that only turns on if the switch is flipped AND a light sensor detects darkness
A stairwell light that turns on if either the top or the bottom switch is flipped
A lamp with a single switch and no other condition

exercises.explain: Right! Both the switch AND the darkness sensor must agree — that's two conditions wired in series, an AND gate.

exercises.prompt: You want a garage light to turn on only when a door sensor detects the door is open AND it is after sunset. How should the two switches be wired?

options.text: In series, so both must be closed (AND) for the light to turn on
In parallel, so either being closed turns on the light
It can't be done with switches

exercises.explain: Exactly! Wiring them in series means the light only turns on when both the door sensor AND the sunset sensor agree. Next: this hardware version of logic worked — but every single wire had to be planned by a human. What if a machine could learn its own rules instead?
